event: Portrait of St. Mark the Evangelist
symbols: halo — sanctity and divine calling of the evangelist
book or codex in hand — the Gospel of Mark, the written word of God
winged lion in red medallion — traditional symbol of Mark derived from Ezekiel's vision and Revelation 4, representing the roaring voice of the wilderness and the royal courage of faith
tree — rootedness in the living tradition and the tree of life
stone wall — the foundation of the Church built upon apostolic witness
description: A profile view of St. Mark the Evangelist depicted as a dark-skinned, bearded man with a large golden halo. He holds a book or codex in his hands and gazes to the left with a contemplative expression. He wears a simple brown robe over a white garment stained with traces of red. Behind him, a tree rises against a pale blue sky, and in a red circular medallion among the branches appears the winged lion, his traditional evangelist symbol. A low stone wall extends behind the figure.
theological_description: St. Mark the Evangelist, traditionally identified as John Mark of the Acts of the Apostles (Acts 12:12, 25; 15:37-39), is the author of the earliest synoptic Gospel. His Gospel opens with the voice crying in the wilderness (Mark 1:3), which the Church Fathers associated with the lion's roar, giving rise to the winged lion as his evangelist symbol -- derived ultimately from the four living creatures of Ezekiel 1:10 and Revelation 4:7. Nesterov's depiction is notable for presenting Mark with distinctly dark features, perhaps reflecting the tradition that Mark founded the Church of Alexandria in Egypt, rooting him in an African context. The book he holds represents the Gospel itself, the written testimony of Christ's life, death, and resurrection as received from the apostle Peter, whose interpreter Mark is traditionally said to have been (per Papias and Eusebius). The winged lion medallion affirms his identity within the four-fold Gospel witness, alongside Matthew (angel), Luke (ox), and John (eagle). Mark's contemplative gaze suggests the evangelist as one who both receives and transmits divine revelation.
